learningGoal: How to understand what a negative number represents in real life
How to see that negative numbers sit below zero on a number line

screens.title: What does -5 mean?
screens.body:
Aisha is checking the temperature outside on a freezing morning in Scotland. The display shows **-5°C**.

But what does that little minus sign mean? Can a number really be **less than zero**?

screens.title: Below zero!
screens.body:
A **negative number** is a number **less than zero**. Zero means water freezes.

So **-5** means **5 degrees below freezing**.

On a number line, negative numbers go to the **left** of zero. The further left, the smaller the number!

interaction.sentence: A negative number is a number less than ____
interaction.correctFeedback: Yes! Negative numbers are less than zero — they sit to the left on the number line. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — negative numbers are less than zero.

screens.title: Where is it on the number line?
screens.body: -5 is on which side of zero?

interaction.question: Where is -5 on the number line?
options: It doesn't go on a number line
interaction.correctFeedback: Exactly! Negative numbers always sit to the **left** of zero on a number line. -5 means 5 degrees below freezing! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Negative numbers go to the **left** of zero. Positive numbers go to the right. -5 is to the left.

screens.title: Negatives are below zero!
screens.body: Negative numbers are just numbers **below zero**. They appear everywhere in real life!

steps.text: Negative numbers are LESS than zero
steps.why: The minus sign tells you it's below zero

steps.text: They go to the LEFT on a number line
Real-life examples: temperature, debt, depth, underground floors
steps.why: You'll see them everywhere once you start looking! ✓

learningGoal: How to see that the number line extends below zero
How to read negative numbers from a number line

screens.title: What do you notice?
screens.body: Look at the number line below. Some numbers have a minus sign and some don't. What do you notice about where each group sits?

screens.title: Two sides of zero
screens.body:
The number line has **two sides**:

- **Left of zero** → negative numbers (with a minus sign)
- **Right of zero** → positive numbers
- **Zero** is right in the middle — it's neither positive nor negative!

screens.title: Which are negative?
screens.body: How many of these numbers are **negative**: -4, -1, 2, 5?

interaction.question: How many of these numbers are negative?
interaction.correctFeedback: Spot on! **2** of those numbers are negative — they're the ones with a minus sign, sitting left of zero! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Count the ones with a minus sign (left of zero). There are **2** negative numbers.

screens.title: The number line goes both ways!
screens.body: The number line doesn't stop at zero — it keeps going left into the negatives. The further left, the smaller the number!

steps.why: These are the numbers you already know: 1, 2, 3...
It's the dividing line between positive and negative ✓

learningGoal: How to count forwards through zero from a negative number
How to count backwards through zero into negatives

screens.title: Count forwards from -3
screens.body:
Lauren starts at **-3** and counts **7 steps forwards**.

What number will Lauren land on? We'll need to count **through zero**!

screens.title: Count step by step
screens.body: Let's count 7 steps forwards from **-3**. Don't skip ahead — just count one number at a time, right through zero. Zero is just another number in the sequence!

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body:
Start at **-2**. Count **6 steps forwards**.

What number do you land on?

interaction.question: Start at -2, count 6 forwards. Where do you land?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! -2 + 6 = **4**. You counted straight through zero! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Count from -2: -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, 3, 4. You land on **4**.

screens.title: Zero is just a stepping stone!
screens.body: When counting through zero, just keep going — zero is just another number in the sequence!

steps.why: Don't stop or skip at zero — just count through it ✓

learningGoal: Why you must not skip zero when counting
How to spot counting errors across zero

screens.title: Did Mia count correctly?
screens.body:
Mia started at **-2** and counted **5 steps forwards**.

Mia says the answer is **4**.

But look at the sequence: -2, -1, 1, 2, 3, 4. Can you spot what went wrong?

screens.title: Zero counts as a number!
screens.body: Mia skipped zero when counting, jumping from -1 straight to 1! Zero is a real number — you must count it as one of your steps. Tap to see the correct sequence on the number line.

screens.title: Now you try a different one!
screens.body:
Start at **-3**, count **6 forwards** (don't skip zero!).

Where do you land?

interaction.question: Start at -3, count 6 forwards. Where?
interaction.correctFeedback: Superstar! The answer is **3**. Zero counts as a step! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Count carefully: -3, -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, 3. The answer is **3**.

screens.title: Don't skip zero!
screens.body: The most common mistake when counting through zero is **skipping it**. Remember:

steps.text: Zero IS a number — it counts as a step

steps.why: That skips a step and makes your answer wrong by 1

steps.text: Count on your fingers if you need to!
steps.why: There's no shame in checking carefully ✓

learningGoal: How to add a positive number to a negative number
Why adding moves you to the RIGHT on a number line

screens.title: What is -8 + 5?
screens.body:
A thermometer in a freezer reads -8°C. Someone opens the door and it warms up by 5°C.

We need to work out **-8 + 5**. Adding a positive number moves us to the **right** on the number line!

screens.title: Move right on the number line
screens.body: Start at **-8** and jump **5** places to the right.

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body:
Start at **-6**. Add **4**.

What do you get?

interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! -6 + 4 = **-2**. You moved right on the number line! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Start at -6 and count 4 to the right: the answer is **-2**.

screens.title: Adding makes you move RIGHT!
screens.body: When you **add a positive** number, you always move to the **right** on the number line — even if you start in the negatives!

steps.why: Even from a negative starting point

learningGoal: Why -5 + 8 is NOT the same as 5 + 8
How to avoid ignoring the negative sign

screens.title: Tom says -5 + 8 = 13
screens.body:
Tom worked out **-5 + 8** and got **13**.

That seems too big! Can you see what went wrong?

screens.title: Don't ignore the minus sign!
screens.body: Tom ignored the minus sign and did 5 + 8 = 13 instead of -5 + 8 = 3. The minus sign changes your starting point completely! Tap to see where you really end up on the number line.

screens.title: Now try this one!
screens.body: What is **-7 + 9**? Remember — don't ignore the minus sign!

interaction.correctFeedback: Superstar! -7 + 9 = **2**. The minus sign matters! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Start at -7 (not 7!) and add 9. Answer: **2**.

screens.title: The minus sign MATTERS!
screens.body: Never ignore a minus sign. **-5 + 8** is completely different from **5 + 8**!

steps.text: Always look at WHERE you start
steps.why: -5 starts way left of zero, not at 5

steps.text: Add by moving RIGHT from the start
Use the number line if unsure
steps.why: Point to your start, count the jumps ✓

learningGoal: How to subtract when the answer goes below zero
Why subtracting moves you LEFT on the number line

screens.title: What is 3 − 8?
screens.body:
A pond is at 3°C on a winter evening. Overnight the temperature drops by 8°C.

We need **3 − 8**. But 8 is bigger than 3! Does that mean the answer is negative?

screens.title: Go through zero!
screens.body: When you subtract more than you have, you go **through zero** into the negatives. Tap to follow the journey step by step!

interaction.correctFeedback: Perfect order! Go to zero first, then keep going into the negatives. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — first go down to zero, then subtract what's left.

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body:
**2 − 6** = ?

You know it goes below zero. How far below?

interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! 2 − 6 = **-4**. You went through zero and kept going! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! From 2, it's 2 to zero, then 4 more: **-4**.

screens.title: Subtracting below zero!
screens.body: If you subtract more than you have, the answer goes **negative**. Just keep counting left on the number line!

steps.why: Work out how much you've used

steps.text: Keep going left with what's left
steps.why: That gives you the negative answer ✓

learningGoal: When to expect a negative answer from subtraction in real life
How to tell if an answer will be negative before calculating

screens.title: Will the answer be negative?
screens.body:
Chloe is checking her pocket money. She has £5 but wants to buy a book costing £9.

**5 − 9** = ?

We're taking away MORE than we started with. What happens?

screens.title: You go below zero!
screens.body:
We need **5 − 9**. Since 9 is **more** than 5, the answer will be **negative** — we go below zero!

**3 rules for subtraction:**
- Subtract **MORE** than you start with → answer is **NEGATIVE** (e.g. 3 − 8 = −5)
- Subtract **LESS** than you start with → answer is **POSITIVE** (e.g. 8 − 3 = 5)
- Subtract the **SAME** amount → answer is **ZERO** (e.g. 5 − 5 = 0)

Tap to see this on the number line.

interaction.correctFeedback: Spot on! 3 − 8 = **-5**. Below zero! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 3 − 8 = **-5**. You go below zero when subtracting a bigger number.

screens.title: Quick check: will it be negative?
screens.body: Remember the 3 rules: subtract more → negative, subtract less → positive, subtract the same → zero. Check the sign before you calculate! ✓

learningGoal: How to find the difference between a negative and a positive number
Why you add the distances from zero

screens.title: What's the difference between -4 and 3?
screens.body:
A weather chart showing the coldest and warmest temperatures in a week. The two values are **-4°C** and **3°C**.

What's the **difference** between them? It's not as simple as subtracting!

screens.title: Add the distances from zero!
screens.body: When two numbers sit on **opposite sides** of zero, finding the gap between them requires a special trick. Tap to discover it!

interaction.correctFeedback: Perfect order! Find each distance from zero, then add them together. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — find each number's distance from zero, then add them.

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: What's the difference between **-5** and **2**?

interaction.question: Difference between -5 and 2?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! 5 + 2 = **7**. Add the distances from zero! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! -5 is 5 from zero, 2 is 2 from zero. Total: **7**.

screens.title: The difference trick!
screens.body: When finding the difference between a negative and a positive number:

steps.text: Find how far the negative number is from zero
Find how far the positive number is from zero

steps.why: Because they're on opposite sides of zero ✓

learningGoal: Why subtracting the numbers gives the wrong answer for differences across zero
How to avoid the difference trap

screens.title: Is the difference really 1?
screens.body:
Ethan says the difference between **-4** and **3** is **1**.

Look at the number line — does 1 seem right?

screens.title: Count the gap!
screens.body: Ethan subtracted 3 from 4 to get 1, instead of adding 4 + 3 = 7. When the numbers are on **opposite sides** of zero, subtracting gives the wrong answer. Tap to see the number line proof!

screens.title: Now try a different pair!
screens.body: What's the difference between **-3** and **5**?

interaction.question: Difference between -3 and 5?
interaction.correctFeedback: Spot on! 3 + 5 = **8**. ADD across zero! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Add the distances: 3 + 5 = **8**.

screens.title: Across zero = ADD!
screens.body: Remember this simple rule:

steps.text: Both on the SAME side of zero → SUBTRACT
On OPPOSITE sides of zero → ADD
Think: how many jumps on the number line?
steps.why: Count the gaps — that's the difference ✓

learningGoal: How to solve temperature rise and fall problems
How to use a number line for temperature changes

screens.title: Edinburgh: -3°C then it rises by 8°C
screens.body:
The temperature in **Edinburgh** is **-3°C** at dawn. During the day it rises by **8°C**.

What is the new temperature?

screens.title: Temperature rises!
screens.body:
**Rises** means it gets **warmer** — move **right** on the number line.

**-3 + 8 = 5°C**

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body:
It's **-5°C** and the temperature rises by **7°C**.

What is the new temperature?

interaction.question: New temperature?
interaction.correctFeedback: Superstar! The new temperature is **2°C**! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! -5 + 7 = **2°C**.

screens.title: Temperature word clues!
screens.body: Look for these key words in temperature problems:

steps.text: Rises / warms up / increases = ADD (move right)
Draw a number line if you're unsure
steps.why: It makes the direction really clear ✓

learningGoal: How to avoid errors with temperature differences
Why crossing zero needs extra care

screens.title: Is the temperature change really 1°C?
screens.body:
Noah says the difference between **-4°C** and **3°C** is **1°C**.

Look at the number line. Does that look right?

screens.title: They cross zero — ADD!
screens.body: Noah subtracted 3 from 4 to get 1, but the temperatures are on opposite sides of zero so you need to ADD: 4 + 3 = 7. When temperatures cross zero, you need to count the **total gap** on the number line. Tap to see the correct approach.

screens.title: What's the correct difference?
screens.body: How many degrees between **-4°C** and **3°C**?

interaction.question: Difference = 4 + 3 = ?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! 4 + 3 = **7°C**. Add across zero! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 4 + 3 = **7°C**.

screens.title: Temperature difference = add across zero!
screens.body: When temperatures cross zero, always ADD the two distances from zero to find the difference.

steps.text: Find how far the cold temperature is from 0°C
Find how far the warm temperature is from 0°C

learningGoal: How to recognise negative numbers in money, depth, and floors
How to solve real-life problems using negatives

screens.title: Negatives in bank account!
screens.body:
Mum is checking her bank account. She's overdrawn by £50, then gets paid £120.

Starting at **-50** and changing by **+120**. Where do we end up?

screens.title: Same maths, different context!
screens.body: No matter the context — money, depth, or floors — the maths is always the same. Tap each step to see how adding works from a negative starting point.

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Back to bank account: start at **-30**, add **75**. What do you get?

interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! -30 + 75 = **45**. Negatives in bank account — no problem! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! -30 + 75 = **45**. Add by moving towards zero and beyond.

screens.title: Negatives are everywhere!
screens.body: Negative numbers pop up in lots of real-life situations. The maths is always the same — you just need to spot the context!

learningGoal: How to read number lines that represent different real-world contexts
How to interpret negative values in context

screens.title: A lift: what do the numbers mean?
screens.body: Look at this number line for **floor numbers**. Zero means **ground floor**. Numbers to the left mean **basements**. Numbers to the right mean **upper floors**.

screens.title: Context gives meaning!
screens.body:
The same number line works for all these contexts. **Zero** is always the **dividing point**.

- **Left of zero** (negative) = basements
- **Right of zero** (positive) = upper floors

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Which floor is 3 below ground?

interaction.question: Which floor is 3 below ground?
interaction.correctFeedback: Spot on! Basements means negative numbers. The answer is **-3**! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Below the reference point = negative. The answer is **-3**.

screens.title: Same maths, different stories!
screens.body: Negative numbers work the same way in every context. Just remember what zero means!

learningGoal: How to subtract a positive number from a negative number
Why subtracting makes a negative number even more negative

screens.body:
A freezer is at -5°C. The temperature drops by another 3°C.

We need **-5 − 3**. We're already negative and subtracting MORE. Which direction do we go?

screens.title: Even further left!
screens.body: Subtracting moves you **left**. When you're already negative, subtracting makes you **more negative** — further from zero!

interaction.correctFeedback: Perfect order! Subtracting from a negative makes it even more negative. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — start at the negative number, then move further left.

interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! -7 − 4 = **-11**. Even further into the negatives! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Start at -7, move 4 left: **-11**.

screens.title: Subtracting from a negative!
screens.body: When you subtract from a negative number, the answer gets **even more negative** — you move further from zero.

steps.text: Subtracting = moving LEFT on the number line
steps.why: Always, no matter where you start

steps.text: Think of it as going deeper
steps.why: Deeper underwater, colder temperature, more debt ✓

learningGoal: Why negative − positive does NOT give a positive answer
How to avoid sign errors when subtracting from negatives

screens.title: Kai says -3 − 4 = 1
screens.body:
Kai worked out **-3 − 4** and got **1**.

But we're subtracting from a negative. Should the answer be more negative or less negative?

screens.title: It should be MORE negative!
screens.body:
Kai did 4 − 3 = 1, ignoring the minus sign on the -3.

When you subtract from a negative, you go **further left** — the answer is MORE negative, not less!

interaction.sentence: When you subtract from a negative number, the answer gets ____ negative
interaction.correctFeedback: Yes! Subtracting moves you further left — more negative, further from zero. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — subtracting from a negative makes it MORE negative, not less.

interaction.correctFeedback: Superstar! -5 − 6 = **-11**. More negative, not less! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Start at -5, move 6 left: **-11**.

screens.title: Don't flip the sign!
screens.body: The biggest trap: thinking a negative minus a positive gives something closer to zero. It doesn't — it goes further away!

steps.text: Think of it as adding the numbers, then making negative
Use the number line to check
steps.why: Start at -3, jump 4 to the left = -7 ✓
